title: Statistical Tagging
concept: Statistical taggers learn tag probabilities from large labeled corpora, assigning the most likely tag sequence using hidden Markov models.

exercises.prompt: What do statistical taggers rely on to decide the part of speech of a word?
exercises.explain: Statistical tagging relies on calculated probabilities and mathematical likelihoods to assign parts of speech.

cards.title: How Do Computers Label Words?
cards.body: How does a computer know if the word 'light' is a noun (like a lamp), a verb (to light a fire), or an adjective (a light box)? It doesn't just guess—it uses math and probabilities from millions of sentences to find the perfect fit!

cards.title: What is Statistical Tagging?
cards.body: Statistical tagging is a method where a computer program reads a massive collection of pre-tagged text (a corpus) to learn the mathematical probability of which words belong to which parts of speech. It uses these probabilities to guess the best tag sequence for new, unseen sentences.

cards.title: The Word Detective
cards.body: Imagine you are a detective reading a coded message. You have a dictionary of how often certain words are used as nouns or verbs. By looking at the words around them and their historical patterns, you can make an educated guess about the hidden meaning of each word. That is exactly how statistical taggers use probabilities to tag text.

cards.title: The Double Agent Word
cards.body: Consider the word 'run'. It can be a noun ('a long run') or a verb ('to run fast'). A statistical tagger calculates that 'run' after 'a' is highly likely to be a noun, whereas 'run' after 'to' is highly likely to be a verb, based on millions of sentences it analyzed during training.

cards.title: Myth: It's Just a Dictionary Check
cards.body: People often think statistical taggers just look up each word in a simple dictionary. In reality, they evaluate the entire sequence of words together, calculating the probability of the whole sentence's structure to make sure the tags make sense as a team.

cards.title: Rules vs. Probabilities
cards.body: Compare how rule-based systems differ from statistical systems that use probability.

right.text: Uses probabilities from real-world data to find the most likely match.

left.text: Uses strict, manual rules written by humans, which can easily break.

exercises.prompt: How does a statistical tagger learn which tag is correct?

options.text: By learning patterns and probabilities from a large text corpus.
By checking a dictionary and ignoring the context of the sentence.
By asking the programmer to type the label for every word.
By choosing a random tag for every word in the sentence.

exercises.explain: Statistical taggers rely on math and patterns learned from large collections of already tagged text, known as a corpus.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A statistical tagger only looks at a word in isolation, completely ignoring the words around it.
exercises.explain: Statistical taggers look at the surrounding words to calculate the best sequence of tags, rather than looking at each word completely on its own.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how statistical taggers make their decisions.
exercises.sentence: Statistical taggers learn tag [blank] from large labeled datasets to guess the correct parts of speech.
exercises.explain: Statistical taggers use probabilities calculated from large datasets to determine the most likely tags.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes a statistical AI tagger rather than a rule-based or manual system?

options.text: A system that calculates the mathematical likelihood of 'play' being a verb based on millions of past sentences.
A system that uses a strict rule saying: 'If a word ends in -ing, it is always a verb.'
A system where a human editor manually highlights and labels every word in a document.

exercises.explain: The first description uses statistics and probabilities based on data, which is how AI statistical taggers work. The second relies on rigid, manually written rules, which is a rule-based tagger.

exercises.prompt: How do statistical taggers determine the correct part of speech for a sentence?

options.text: They look at the probability of tag patterns and word histories to pick the most likely tag sequence.
They strictly look up words in a dictionary and ignore the surrounding context.
They ask a human user to manually tag every single sentence in real-time.
They randomly assign tags to words until the sentence looks grammatically correct.

exercises.explain: Statistical taggers use probabilities calculated from huge collections of text (corpora) to determine the most likely sequence of parts of speech (tags) for a sentence, often using Hidden Markov Models to calculate these likelihoods.
